content:
# John Chapter 6 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in John Chapter 6 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 6:2 — "signs that he was doing on the sick" vs. "miraculous signs he performed on the sick"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "they were seeing the signs that he was doing upon the sick" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "they saw the signs that he was doing on the sick" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "they saw the miraculous signs he performed on the sick" | Dynamic; adds "miraculous" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA again adds "miraculous" to σημεῖα — John's deliberate use of "signs" (not "miracles") must be preserved.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Recurring pattern.

---

## 6:3 — "the mountain" vs. "a hillside" for Greek τὸ ὄρος

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Yeshua went up on the mountain" | Literal with article |
| LOGOS | "Jesus went up on the mountain" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "Jesus climbed a hillside" | Dynamic; "hillside" diminishes; "climbed" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. τὸ ὄρος with the article suggests a specific, known mountain. PNEUMA's "hillside" reduces its significance.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:5 — "Where are we to buy bread" vs. "Where can we buy bread to feed all these people?"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "From where shall we buy bread that these may eat?" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "Where are we to buy bread, so that these people may eat?" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "Where can we buy bread to feed all these people?" | Dynamic; "feed all these people" compresses |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. Minor divergence; all three are acceptable.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minimal divergence.

---

## 6:7 — "Two hundred denarii worth of bread" vs. "Even if we worked for months"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Two hundred denarii of bread is not sufficient for them" | Literal; retains denarii |
| LOGOS | "Two hundred denarii worth of bread would not be enough for each of them to get a little." | Formal; retains denarii |
| PNEUMA | "Even if we worked for months, we wouldn't have enough money to feed them!" | Dynamic; converts denarii to time-equivalent; adds exclamation |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's conversion to "months of wages" is a valid interpretive move (200 denarii ~ 8 months' wages) but the original currency reference should be preserved in the translation text, with explanation in notes.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:10 — "Have the people sit down" vs. "Make the people recline" vs. "Tell everyone to sit down"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Make the people recline." | Literal; "recline" for ἀναπεσεῖν |
| LOGOS | "Have the people sit down." | Formal; English equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "'Tell everyone to sit down,' Jesus said." | Dynamic; adds narrative attribution |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. The Greek ἀναπεσεῖν means "to recline" (the usual posture for eating), but "sit down" is the natural English equivalent. PNEUMA adds unnecessary attribution.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:14 — "the Prophet who is to come" vs. "the Prophet we have been expecting"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "This is truly the Prophet, the [one] coming into the world." | Literal |
| LOGOS | "This is indeed the Prophet who is to come into the world!" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "This is truly the Prophet we have been expecting!" | Dynamic; "we have been expecting" interprets |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "we have been expecting" is interpretive — the Greek says "the one coming into the world" (ὁ ἐρχόμενος εἰς τὸν κόσμον), which has theological weight beyond mere "expectation."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:15 — "withdrew again to the mountain by himself" vs. "slipped away into the hills"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "withdrew again to the mountain, himself alone" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "Jesus withdrew again to the mountain by himself" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "he slipped away into the hills by himself" | Dynamic; "slipped away" / "hills" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. "Slipped away" adds a furtive connotation. "Hills" (plural) differs from "the mountain" (singular, with article).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:19 — "three or four miles" vs. "twenty-five or thirty stadia"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having rowed about twenty-five or thirty stadia" | Literal; ancient measurement |
| LOGOS | "When they had rowed about three or four miles" | Formal; converts to miles |
| PNEUMA | "They had rowed three or four miles" | Dynamic; same conversion |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. Converting stadia to miles aids comprehension (1 stadion ~ 607 feet). Both LOGOS and PNEUMA make the same conversion.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 6:20 — "It is I" vs. "I am" vs. "I AM" for Greek ἐγώ εἰμι

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I am; do not fear." | Preserves raw ἐγώ εἰμι; "fear" |
| LOGOS | "It is I; do not be afraid." | Standard formal rendering; "afraid" |
| PNEUMA | "I AM. Don't be afraid." | Capitalizes to signal divine name; colloquial |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "It is I." This rendering works on both levels (self-identification and divine echo). EMET's bare "I am" is more literal. PNEUMA's capitalized "I AM" forces a theological reading that the Greek leaves ambiguous.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Significant interpretive divergence.

---

## 6:26 — "you ate your fill of the loaves" vs. "I fed you"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you ate of the loaves and were filled" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "you ate your fill of the loaves" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "you want to be with me because I fed you, not because you understood the signs" | Dynamic; "I fed you"; "understood" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA converts "you saw signs" (εἴδετε σημεῖα) to "you understood the signs" — changing seeing to comprehension. Also restructures the sentence significantly.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:27 — "Do not work for the food that perishes" vs. "Don't waste your energy striving for perishable food"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Work not for the food perishing" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "Do not work for the food that perishes" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "Don't waste your energy striving for perishable food" | Dynamic; "waste your energy striving" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "waste your energy striving" adds interpretive color not in the Greek imperative μὴ ἐργάζεσθε.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:29 — "This is the work of God" vs. "This is the only work God wants from you"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "This is the work of the God, that you believe in the [one] whom that [one] sent." | Literal |
| LOGOS | "This is the work of God, that you believe in him whom he has sent." | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "This is the only work God wants from you: believe in the one he has sent." | Dynamic; adds "only" and "wants from you" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "only" (not in the Greek) and "wants from you" which reframes the statement.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:35 — "shall not hunger...shall never thirst" vs. "will never be hungry again...will never be thirsty"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The [one] coming to me will not hunger, and the [one] believing in me will never thirst." | Literal |
| LOGOS | "whoever comes to me shall not hunger, and whoever believes in me shall never thirst" | Formal; "shall" |
| PNEUMA | "Whoever comes to me will never be hungry again. Whoever believes in me will never be thirsty." | Dynamic; adds "again"; splits into two sentences |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. The "shall" register is appropriate for this solemn declaration. PNEUMA adds "again" to the first clause.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:37 — "I will never cast out" vs. "I will never reject them"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [one] coming to me I will not cast out" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "whoever comes to me I will never cast out" | Formal; "never" with emphatic double negative |
| PNEUMA | "I will never reject them" | Dynamic; "reject" instead of "cast out" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. "Cast out" (ἐκβάλω) is more literal and more vivid than PNEUMA's "reject." The image of being thrown out versus merely refused is significant.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:44 — "unless the Father...draws him" vs. "unless the Father...draws them to me"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "unless the Father having sent me draws him" | Literal; singular "him" |
| LOGOS | "unless the Father who sent me draws him" | Formal; singular |
| PNEUMA | "unless the Father who sent me draws them to me" | Dynamic; plural "them"; adds "to me" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. The Greek uses the singular (αὐτόν). PNEUMA shifts to plural and adds "to me."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:51 — "he will live forever" vs. "he will live into the age" for Greek ζήσει εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he will live into the age" | Literal; "into the age" |
| LOGOS | "he will live forever" | Formal; standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "will live forever" | Dynamic; same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS/PNEUMA. "Forever" is the standard English rendering of εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα.

**Status:** Consensus between LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 6:52 — "disputed among themselves" vs. "began arguing with each other"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Ioudaioi were disputing with one another" | Literal; "disputing" |
| LOGOS | "The Jews then disputed among themselves" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "the people began arguing with each other about what he meant" | Dynamic; "people" instead of "Jews"; adds "about what he meant" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA replaces "Jews" with "people" and adds the explanatory "about what he meant."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:54-58 — "feeds on" vs. "eats" vs. "eating" for Greek τρώγων

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The [one] eating the flesh of me..." (uses "eating" for τρώγω) | Literal; does not distinguish τρώγω from ἐσθίω |
| LOGOS | "Whoever feeds on my flesh..." (uses "feeds on" for τρώγω) | Formal; distinguishes τρώγω as more visceral |
| PNEUMA | "anyone who eats my flesh..." (uses "eats" throughout) | Dynamic; no distinction |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "feeds on." John deliberately shifts from ἐσθίω ("eat," used in vv.49-53) to τρώγω ("munch/gnaw/feed on," used in vv.54-58). This intensification is deliberate and theologically significant — the language becomes more physical and confrontational. LOGOS alone preserves this distinction.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Significant lexical distinction.

---

## 6:60 — "This is a hard saying; who can listen to it?" vs. "This is very hard to understand. How can anyone accept it?"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "This word is hard; who is able to hear it?" | Literal; "hear" for ἀκούειν |
| LOGOS | "This is a hard saying; who can listen to it?" | Formal; "listen" |
| PNEUMA | "This is very hard to understand. How can anyone accept it?" | Dynamic; "very hard to understand"; "accept" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. σκληρός means "hard/harsh" — not "hard to understand." The saying is offensive, not incomprehensible. PNEUMA's "very hard to understand" shifts from offense to difficulty, and "accept" replaces the sensory "listen/hear."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:61 — "Do you take offense at this?" vs. "Does this offend you?" vs. "Does this scandalize you?"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Does this scandalize you?" | "Scandalize" for σκανδαλίζει |
| LOGOS | "Do you take offense at this?" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "Does this offend you?" | Dynamic; simpler |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. EMET's "scandalize" is etymologically faithful (σκανδαλίζω = to cause to stumble/be scandalized) but slightly archaic.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor divergence.

---

## 6:63 — "The flesh is no help at all" vs. "Human effort accomplishes nothing"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the flesh profits nothing" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "the flesh is no help at all" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "Human effort accomplishes nothing" | Dynamic; interprets "flesh" as "human effort" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. "Flesh" (σάρξ) must be preserved here — it is a key Johannine term. PNEUMA's "human effort" is one possible interpretation but narrows the meaning.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:64 — "who would betray him" vs. "who would deliver him up" for Greek παραδώσων

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "who is the [one] who will deliver him up" | Literal; "deliver up" |
| LOGOS | "who it was who would betray him" | Formal; "betray" |
| PNEUMA | "he knew who would betray him" | Dynamic; same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. παραδίδωμι literally means "hand over/deliver up" but "betray" captures the moral dimension in this context.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor divergence.

---

## 6:66 — "turned back and no longer walked with him" vs. "turned away and deserted him"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "went away to the [things] behind and were no longer walking with him" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "many of his disciples turned back and no longer walked with him" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "many of his disciples turned away and deserted him" | Dynamic; "deserted" — stronger |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "deserted" adds emotional weight not in the Greek, which simply says they "went away to the things behind" (εἰς τὰ ὀπίσω) and no longer walked with him.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:70 — "one of you is a devil" vs. "one is a devil"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "of you, one is a devil" | Literal; Greek word order |
| LOGOS | "yet one of you is a devil" | Formal; adds "yet" |
| PNEUMA | "one is a devil" | Dynamic; drops "of you" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. The "yet" captures the adversative force. All three preserve "a devil" (διάβολος) rather than softening it.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor divergence.

---

# Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive expansion (adding words) | 11 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| PNEUMA dynamic paraphrase (restructuring) | 8 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| EMET literal structure / transliteration | 7 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| Lexical distinction (τρώγω vs. ἐσθίω) | 1 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS (significant) |
| ἐγώ εἰμι rendering | 1 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| "Signs" vs. "miraculous signs" | 1 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS (recurring) |

## Overall Pattern Analysis

Chapter 6, the longest in John's Gospel, tests each translator's consistency across an extended discourse. The most significant divergence is the τρώγω/ἐσθίω distinction in vv.54-58: LOGOS alone preserves John's deliberate shift from "eat" to the more visceral "feed on," which is crucial to the escalating shock of the Bread of Life discourse. PNEUMA's rendering of ἐγώ εἰμι as "I AM" (capitalized) in v.20 represents a significant interpretive choice — forcing a theophanic reading that LOGOS leaves open. PNEUMA continues the pattern of adding "miraculous" to "signs," which undermines John's careful vocabulary. All three translators agree on the key christological title "Holy One of God" in v.69, following the distinctive Sinaiticus reading.

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
